content.title: 下雨 (xià yǔ) – To Rain
content.body:
The phrase **下雨** (xià yǔ) means "to rain" or "raining." It combines 下 (xià, to fall/descend) and 雨 (yǔ, rain).

Example: 今天下雨。(Jīntiān xià yǔ.) – "It's raining today."

content.title: Talking About Health and Discomfort
content.body:
To describe feeling unwell in Chinese, common patterns include:

**General discomfort:**
- 我不舒服。(Wǒ bù shūfu.) – "I don't feel well."

**Specific pain:**
- 我 + [body part] + 很疼。
- Example: 我头很疼。(Wǒ tóu hěn téng.) – "My head hurts a lot." / "I have a bad headache."

The structure places the body part

content.title: 冷 (lěng) – Cold
content.body:
The word **冷** (lěng) means "cold" and describes low temperature or cold weather.

Example: 今天很冷。(Jīntiān hěn lěng.) – "Today is very cold."

content.title: Connecting Weather and Health
content.body:
In Chinese conversation, weather and health are often connected. Cold weather might make you uncomfortable, or hot weather might affect how you feel:

- 今天很冷，我不舒服。(Jīntiān hěn lěng, wǒ bù shūfu.) – "Today is very cold, I don't feel well."
- 天气很热。(Tiānqì hěn rè.) – "The weather is very hot."

The com

content.title: Daily Health Check Scenario
content.body:
In Chinese culture, asking about someone's health and the weather are common conversation starters. These exchanges demonstrate social care:

**Scenario elements:**
- Greeting and asking about health: 你好吗？/ 你身体好吗？
- Responding about health: 我很好。/ 我不舒服。
- Discussing weather: 今天天气怎么样？
- Describing sym

content.title: 热 (rè) – Hot
content.body:
The word **热** (rè) means "hot" and describes high temperature or warm weather.

Example: 今天很热。(Jīntiān hěn rè.) – "Today is very hot."

content.title: Medical Contexts: 医院 and 医生
content.body:
The words 医院 (yīyuàn, hospital) and 医生 (yīshēng, doctor) are frequently used together when discussing medical situations:

- 我去医院。(Wǒ qù yīyuàn.) – "I'm going to the hospital."
- 他是医生。(Tā shì yīshēng.) – "He is a doctor."
- 我要看医生。(Wǒ yào kàn yīshēng.) – "I want to see a doctor."

The verb 看 (kàn, to

content.title: 头 (tóu) – Head
content.body:
The word **头** (tóu) means "head." It's commonly used when describing headaches: 头疼 (tóu téng).

Example: 我的头很大。(Wǒ de tóu hěn dà.) – "My head is very big."

content.title: Medical Visit Narrative
content.body:
**Narrative:**

我今天不舒服。我的头很疼，眼睛也不舒服。今天天气很冷，下雪。我不去学校，我去医院。医生很好。

content.title: 不舒服 (bù shūfu) – Unwell/Uncomfortable
content.body:
The phrase **不舒服** (bù shūfu) means "unwell," "uncomfortable," or "not feeling well." It combines 不 (bù, not) and 舒服 (shūfu, comfortable).

Example: 我不舒服。(Wǒ bù shūfu.) – "I don't feel well."

content.title: Weather Question Pattern: 天气怎么样？
content.body:
To ask "How is the weather?" in Chinese, use: **...天气怎么样？** (...tiānqì zěnmeyàng?)

Most commonly: 今天天气怎么样？(Jīntiān tiānqì zěnmeyàng?) – "How is the weather today?"

The pattern is: [time] + 天气 + 怎么样？

content.title: Body Parts Vocabulary Set
content.body:
Key HSK 1 body parts include:

- 头 (tóu) – head
- 眼睛 (yǎnjing) – eyes

These are often used with descriptive adjectives:
- 大 (dà) – big
- 小 (xiǎo) – small

Or in health contexts:
- 疼 (téng) – hurt/pain

Example: 我的眼睛很小。(Wǒ de yǎnjing hěn xiǎo.) – "My eyes are very small."

content.title: 身体 (shēntǐ) – Body/Health
content.body:
The word **身体** (shēntǐ) means "body" or "health." When asking about someone's health, Chinese speakers often ask about their 身体.

Example: 你身体好吗？(Nǐ shēntǐ hǎo ma?) – "Are you in good health?"

content.title: 眼睛 (yǎnjing) – Eyes
content.body:
The word **眼睛** (yǎnjing) means "eyes." Note that the second syllable is often pronounced in neutral tone in everyday speech.

Example: 她的眼睛很大。(Tā de yǎnjing hěn dà.) – "Her eyes are very big."

content.title: Describing Symptoms and Seeking Help
content.body:
When describing health problems in Chinese, it's important to combine what you've learned about:
- Identifying the problem (不舒服, specific pain with 疼)
- Naming body parts (头, 眼睛)
- Explaining actions (去医院, 看医生)
- Providing context (weather, time)

A complete health-related communication might includ

content.title: 疼 (téng) – To Hurt/Pain
content.body:
The word **疼** (téng) means "to hurt," "to ache," or "pain." It's commonly used to describe pain: 头疼 (tóu téng, headache).

Example: 我的头很疼。(Wǒ de tóu hěn téng.) – "My head hurts a lot."

content.title: Describing Weather Conditions
content.body:
Weather descriptions in Chinese typically follow this pattern:

[time] + 天气 + 很 + [adjective]
OR
[time] + [weather verb]

Examples:
- 今天天气很热。(Jīntiān tiānqì hěn rè.) – "Today's weather is very hot."
- 今天很冷。(Jīntiān hěn lěng.) – "Today is very cold."
- 今天下雨。(Jīntiān xià yǔ.) – "It's raining today."

content.title: 医院 (yīyuàn) – Hospital
content.body:
The word **医院** (yīyuàn) means "hospital." It combines 医 (yī, medicine/medical) and 院 (yuàn, institution/courtyard).

Example: 我去医院。(Wǒ qù yīyuàn.) – "I'm going to the hospital."

content.title: Weather Report and Response
content.body:
**Dialogue:**

A: 今天天气怎么样？
B: 今天很冷，下雪。
A: 你身体好吗？
B: 我不舒服，头很疼。
A: 你去医院吗？
B: 我去医院看医生。

content.title: 下雪 (xià xuě) – To Snow
content.body:
The phrase **下雪** (xià xuě) means "to snow" or "snowing." It combines 下 (xià, to fall/descend) and 雪 (xuě, snow).

Example: 今天下雪。(Jīntiān xià xuě.) – "It's snowing today."

content.title: 医生 (yīshēng) – Doctor
content.body:
The word **医生** (yīshēng) means "doctor." It combines 医 (yī, medicine/medical) and 生 (shēng, person/to give birth).

Example: 他是医生。(Tā shì yīshēng.) – "He is a doctor."

content.title: 天气 (tiānqì) – Weather
content.body:
The word **天气** (tiānqì) means "weather." It combines 天 (tiān, sky/day) and 气 (qì, air/atmosphere).

Common usage: 今天天气怎么样？(Jīntiān tiānqì zěnmeyàng?) – "How is the weather today?"
